quiz.question: If your investments dropped 20% in a month, what would you do?

answers.balanced: Wait and see — stick to the plan
answers.adventurous: Buy more while prices are low

demo.title: Welcome to Future You
demo.lead: This is a preview of the app shell — the sidebar, status rail, and bottom tab bar that wrap every screen. The real pages arrive next.
demo.cardPlanBody: Once you answer a few questions, your personalized mix lands here — with the why behind every choice.
demo.cardLearnBody: Short, plain-language lessons turn each part of your plan into something you understand.
demo.howProfileBody: A few quick questions about your situation.
demo.howGoalBody: What you're saving toward, and by when.
demo.howRiskBody: How much ups and downs you're okay with.

StyleGuide.title: Design tokens
StyleGuide.intro: The building blocks of Future You — colour, type, shape, and motion. This page is a dev-only reference.
StyleGuide.colorNote: Bold colour on the actionable things; calm cream and white everywhere else. Money never relies on colour alone.
StyleGuide.twoGreenNote: One green can't be both a button fill and readable text at WCAG-AA. So green-600 fills buttons with white text (5.22:1), and green-700 carries any green text or icon on a light background (6.39:1).
StyleGuide.typeNote: Baloo 2 for chunky, friendly headings; Nunito for legible body text and numerals.
StyleGuide.specimen: Invest a little, learn a lot, and watch Future You grow.
StyleGuide.buttonNote: Press it — the hard 4px shadow sinks flat. Our one tactile signature.
StyleGuide.chipNote: Tap to choose — used in the risk quiz and interests.
StyleGuide.numbersNote: Money uses tabular lining figures so columns line up, formatted for your language.
StyleGuide.focusNote: Tab to any control for a visible green focus ring. Every transition respects reduced-motion.
StyleGuide.sproutNote: Your guide on (nearly) every screen — used big, with one distinct pose per moment. Sprout breathes with a soft idle bob and holds still if you prefer reduced motion.
StyleGuide.galleryNote: All 109 pieces, each with bilingual alt text — so nothing ever renders without a description for screen readers.

components.title: Components
components.intro: The reusable building blocks, re-skinned onto the tokens above. Each is shown in the states you'll meet in the product.
components.buttonsNote: Our one tactile signature: a hard 4px edge that sinks flat on press. Every label passes AA — white on the dark fills, ink on the light ones.
components.buttonA11y: White text sits only on the dark fills (green, ink); the light fills (coral, gold, sky, grape) carry ink labels.
components.cardsNote: White by default; soft accent washes for variety; one dark ink→grape highlight card per page for a single call-out.
components.cardDefaultBody: A plain white card — the everyday unit of content.
components.cardSkyBody: A soft sky wash for a gentle, informational aside.
components.cardGrapeBody: A grape wash for variety and tertiary notes.
components.cardCoralBody: A coral wash to warmly draw the eye.
components.cardHighlightBody: The one dark highlight a page may use — white text clears AA across the whole gradient.
components.chipsNote: Tap to choose. Selected chips fill with the green tint, gain a green border and a check, and give a small bounce (silent under reduced motion).
components.chipsRiskLabel: How do you feel about risk?
components.chipsInterestsLabel: What are you curious about? (choose any)
components.controlsNote: Friendly 48px fields, a CHF money field with tabular figures, and a segmented control for the short / medium / long horizon.
components.badgesNote: Small labels, and the red count counters from the Learn and Research menus.
components.overlaysNote: Dialogs, sheets, tooltips, tabs and accordions — over a warm ink scrim, focus-trapped, keyboard-friendly, and quiet under reduced motion.
components.ovDialogBody: We'll ask a few quick questions, then show a plan you can actually understand — and change anytime.
components.ovSheetDesc: Jump to another part of Future You.
components.ovTabOverviewBody: A pill tab track: the active tab is a raised white pill, never colour alone.
components.ovTabHoldingsBody: Eight funds, diversified across regions and sectors.
components.ovTabAboutBody: A plain-language summary of what this plan does and why.
components.ovAccA1: Your returns earn returns. Each year's growth joins next year's base, so a small amount can snowball over a long horizon.
components.ovAccA2: Spreading money across many holdings so one bad surprise can't sink the whole plan — many baskets, not one.
components.ovAccQ3: Why hold bonds as well as stocks?
components.ovAccA3: Bonds usually swing less than stocks, so they steady the ride when markets dip.

TeachingPrimitives.title: How Future You explains things
TeachingPrimitives.intro: Two building blocks for learning as you invest — the answer in plain words, with the maths one tap away, and any jargon defined on the spot.
TeachingPrimitives.equationNote: Every number leads with a plain-language answer. Open "Show the maths" only if you want to see exactly how it's worked out.
TeachingPrimitives.eqResult: Start with CHF 1,000, add CHF 100 a month, and if it grows about 5% a year, in 20 years you'd have about CHF 43,800 — even though you only put in CHF 25,000 yourself.
TeachingPrimitives.eqFormulaAlt: Future value equals P times one plus r, all raised to the power n, plus C times the quantity one plus r to the power n minus one, divided by r.
TeachingPrimitives.eqSymFV: Future value — what your savings could grow to.
TeachingPrimitives.eqSymP: Principal — what you start with today (CHF 1,000).
TeachingPrimitives.eqSymC: Contribution — what you add each month (CHF 100).
TeachingPrimitives.eqSymR: Monthly growth rate — your ~5% a year, split across 12 months.
TeachingPrimitives.eqSymN: Number of months you stay invested (20 years = 240).
TeachingPrimitives.eqExampleAlt: Using the example numbers, the future value works out to about 43,800 Swiss francs.
TeachingPrimitives.eqExampleCaption: You put in CHF 25,000 of your own — the other ~CHF 18,800 is growth doing the work.
TeachingPrimitives.eqWhy: Each year's growth earns its own growth the next year — that snowball is compound interest, and time is what makes it big.
TeachingPrimitives.glossaryIntro: Any underlined word has a plain-language definition — tap it to see it, without leaving the page.
TeachingPrimitives.glossaryParagraph: An <etf>ETF</etf> lets you own a whole basket of investments in one go. Spreading your money this way — <diversification>diversification</diversification> — softens the blow when any single holding drops. It won't remove <volatility>volatility</volatility>, the normal up-and-down swing of prices, but it makes the ride smoother.
TeachingPrimitives.defEtf: A single fund you can buy that holds a whole basket of investments at once — instant variety, at low cost.
TeachingPrimitives.defDiversification: Spreading your money across many investments so one bad apple can't sink everything.
TeachingPrimitives.defVolatility: How much an investment's price swings up and down — bigger swings mean a bumpier ride.

Charts.title: Charts that tell the truth
Charts.intro: Every chart answers a plain question, shows a range instead of a false promise, and can open its maths or turn into a table. The figures here are a sample — your real numbers arrive with your plan.
Charts.viewGroup: Switch between the chart and a table of the same numbers

projection.title: Where could my savings grow?
projection.caption: The green line is the most likely path; the shaded band is the range from a rough run to a good one. The further ahead you look, the wider that range — and the more reaching your goal depends on time.
projection.ariaLabel: A projection of your savings over time: a most-likely line inside a shaded range of good and rough outcomes, with your goal and your starting point marked. The same data is available as a table.
projection.legendRange: Range of outcomes (80% of the time)
projection.tableCaption: The same projection as a table: for each year, a rough run, the most likely amount, and a good run.
projection.eqResult: Starting with CHF 10,000 and adding CHF 300 a month, if it grows about 5% a year, in 15 years the most likely path lands near CHF 100,000 — but the real result lands somewhere across the shaded band.
projection.eqFormulaAlt: Future value equals P times one plus r, all raised to the power n, plus C times the quantity one plus r to the power n minus one, divided by r.
projection.eqSymFV: Future value — the most likely amount your savings could grow to.
projection.eqSymP: Principal — what you start with today (CHF 10,000).
projection.eqSymC: Contribution — what you add each month (CHF 300).
projection.eqSymR: Monthly growth rate — about 5% a year, split across 12 months.
projection.eqSymN: Number of months you stay invested (15 years = 180).
projection.eqExampleAlt: Using the example numbers, the future value works out to about 100,000 Swiss francs.
projection.eqExampleCaption: This is only the middle line. The band around it comes from how much returns can vary year to year — which is why we never show a single promise.
projection.eqWhyLabel: Why a band, not a line
projection.eqWhy: Nobody knows the exact return each year, so a single line would be a false promise. The band shows the range most outcomes fall in — honest about what we can and can't know.

drawdown.title: How far could it fall along the way?
drawdown.caption: Even a strong plan dips. This is the “stomach test”: how far the path fell from its most recent high — the deepest fall you'd have had to sit through — before climbing back. The growth chart shows how high it could go; this shows the bumps to hold through on the way.
drawdown.ariaLabel: An underwater plot of how far the projected path falls from its most recent high over time: a soft area dipping below a 0% line, with the deepest fall marked. The same data is available as a table.
drawdown.legendFall: How far below its recent peak
drawdown.tableCaption: The deepest dip in each year: how far the path was below its most recent high, its value then, and that recent peak.
drawdown.eqResult: In this sample the deepest fall was about {mdd} — the path slid from a peak of {peak} to {trough} before climbing back. That worst drop from a recent high is the maximum drawdown.
drawdown.eqFormulaAlt: Maximum drawdown equals the smallest value over time of V sub t divided by V peak — the highest value reached so far — minus one.
drawdown.eqSymV: The invested value at a moment in time.
drawdown.eqSymPeak: The highest value reached up to that moment — the running peak.
drawdown.eqSymMdd: Maximum drawdown — the deepest fall from a peak, as a share of that peak.
drawdown.eqExampleAlt: The trough value divided by the peak value, minus one, is about the maximum drawdown percentage.
drawdown.eqWhy: It's the stomach test — not the final return, but the hardest stretch you'd have to hold through without selling. Bigger, longer-held plans usually see deeper dips and still recover.
